topic: Part 5 Section 5.0 ks-april-2026-update paragraph rewrite (EXP-193 integration) — new two-paragraph structure separating 'Credit the criterion' (paragraph 1) from 'What the criterion does to this page' (paragraph 2), resolving c1 hole #4 (framing asymmetry).
our_argument_summary: EXP-193 replaced the single 145-word paragraph containing the phrase 'strengthened for the author and relaxed for the test' with two 305-word paragraphs. Paragraph 1 ('Credit the criterion') explicitly credits the new dome rule — 'genuinely pre-registered, mechanically discriminating, and not already covered by a competing explanation' — as 'methodological strengthening' over the old 'any single confirmed row falsifies the globe' rule. Paragraph 2 ('What the criterion does to this page') then argues that, applied honestly to the six rows on docs/killshot.html, the new rule removes most of the rows: Sydney-Perth fails the mechanically-discriminating prong (calibration to a known railway distance); Polaris fails the not-already-covered prong (0.27° excess inside refraction noise). Paragraph 2 concludes that 'the rows that survive are roughly the ones our review had already classified as Std Model Explains or Refuted by Data' and that the page converts 'from falsification instrument to methodology-of-good-evidence note.' The rest of the ks-april-2026-update section (credit where due, before/after tables, post-window pattern, CSS-class mismatch, companion OTS concession) is unchanged.

relevant_files: data/sections.json part5.html (new two-paragraph block inside <details id='ks-april-2026-update'>)
dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline:
No automated pipeline — pure prose rewrite of one

-level paragraph replaced by two

-level paragraphs. No numerical claims added beyond the quoted dome rule text.
data_sources_used: quoted dome rule text (new rule body, from chg-20260417-1620-006 verbatim)
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: Paragraph 2 ends with 'applied honestly' which implicitly reserves the claim that the author has NOT yet applied the rule honestly. This is defensible but worth noting as our own load-bearing qualifier.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None — the rewrite is prose-only and does not touch any code_analysis tag counts. The quoted dome rule text matches chg-20260417-1620-006 verbatim (already verified in SEC-5.0.c1 citation_check).

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: Section 5.0 is meta-commentary on a dated dome author edit. monitor.py has no logic touching this section. Tag unchanged from c1.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: Meta-commentary on rhetorical/rule changes, not a cosmological claim. Tag unchanged from c1.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Not a prediction. Section documents a 2026-04-17 author edit; our rewrite was authored 2026-04-20/21. Tag unchanged from c1.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: Not a derivation section. Tag unchanged from c1.

holes_found.description: Paragraph 2 contains a 'survive' ambiguity that a dome defender can attack as internally contradictory with our own verdict table. The key sentence reads: 'Under the tighter rule, the rows that survive are roughly the ones our review had already classified as Std Model Explains or Refuted by Data — i.e., the rows we were already arguing should never have been kill-shots in the first place.' The natural reading of 'survive the tighter rule' is 'pass the rule's three-prong test and remain kill-shot candidates.' Under that reading, the sentence is self-contradictory: rows we classified 'Std Model Explains' (JFK-LHR, SAA, Eclipse, per the Kill-Shot Verdict Summary table in this same part) are literally 'already covered by a competing explanation,' which is precisely what the new rule's third prong EXCLUDES. Our own verdict says those rows fail the new rule — they cannot simultaneously be classified 'Std Model Explains' by us AND 'survive the tighter rule' by the author's reading of his own criterion. A careful defender will quote our paragraph 2 against our Kill-Shot Verdict Summary table and say: 'Your own verdict table says these rows ARE covered by a competing explanation. By your own logic, the new rule removes them. They cannot also survive the new rule. Your argument is inverted.' The intended meaning is almost certainly: 'the four PENDING rows the author did NOT demote on 2026-04-17 are roughly the rows we had classified as Std Model Explains or Refuted by Data, and an honest application of the new rule would remove those too.' But the prose uses 'survive the tighter rule' instead of 'survived the 2026-04-17 edit,' which inverts the semantic direction. NOTE: this ambiguity traces back to SEC-5.0.c1 hole #4's recommendation wording ('the rows that survive the new rule are roughly the rows we already classified as Std Model Explains or Refuted by Data'), which EXP-193 implemented faithfully. The issue is inherited from c1, not introduced by EXP-193 — but it is still a live exposure in the published prose.
holes_found.recommendation: Rewrite the survive-sentence to remove the ambiguity. Suggested replacement: 'The four rows the 2026-04-17 edit left untouched — JFK-LHR, SAA, Eclipse, NMP — are precisely the rows our review had already classified as Std Model Explains or Refuted by Data. An honest application of the new rule removes those too: rows covered by a competing explanation fail the third prong, and a row whose self-reported error is 39.9% is not confirming anything. In other words, the author has partially applied his own new rule (removing Sydney-Perth and Polaris) and the rows he has not yet re-examined are exactly the rows we were already arguing should never have been kill-shots.' This (a) replaces the ambiguous 'survive' verb with the unambiguous 'left untouched' / 'not yet re-examined' framing, (b) commits to the concrete claim that the new rule removes the four PENDING rows, (c) closes the verdict-table consistency loop.

holes_found.description: Paragraph 1's credit-the-criterion framing silently accepts the fact that the 2026-04-17 rewrite simultaneously DELETED the dome-falsification commitment. The before/after rule text (already in the section's own body) shows this: the OLD rule said 'If any single test confirms, the globe model is falsified. If any single test fails, the dome model is falsified.' — two explicit commitments, one per direction. The NEW rule says only 'A clean result only counts as a major discriminator if the test is genuinely pre-registered, mechanically discriminating, and not already covered by a competing explanation. Supportive, calibration-heavy, or non-discriminating rows should not be turned into global cosmology knockouts by slogan alone.' The new rule specifies what counts as a discriminator but is silent on what the discriminator's success or failure implies about the dome — the old commitment that 'if any single test fails, the dome model is falsified' has vanished. Our paragraph 1 credits the new rule for its criteria on what counts as 'decisive against a global model' and characterizes the rewrite as 'methodological strengthening.' But an author who simultaneously tightens the inbound standard AND removes the outbound self-falsification commitment has not strengthened his epistemic position — he has traded a bold-but-loose commitment for a careful-but-one-sided commitment. A dome defender will welcome our unqualified credit in paragraph 1: it lets him quote our own review as agreeing the new rule is 'methodological strengthening' while silently benefiting from the fact that the new rule also gave him an escape hatch from dome-falsifiability. By crediting only the inbound (globe-falsification) half of the rewrite and saying nothing about the outbound (dome-falsification) half that was deleted, we leave a significant unfalsifiability move uncredited as a concern.
holes_found.recommendation: Add one sentence to paragraph 1 flagging the asymmetry. Suggested insertion after 'and we should say so plainly.': 'One asymmetry deserves flagging: the rewrite tightened what counts as a discriminator against the globe while silently removing the old rule\'s symmetric commitment that "if any single test fails, the dome model is falsified." The new rule addresses inbound discrimination only; the author\'s own falsifiability commitment was not preserved in the rewrite. We credit the tighter discriminator-standard; we note that a tighter rule with no dome-falsifiability clause is a narrower rule, not a strictly stronger one.' This preserves paragraph 1's credit-where-due framing while foreclosing the defender's 'see, the critics admit the rule is strengthened' move.

holes_found.description: Paragraph 2's claim 'Applied honestly to the six rows on docs/killshot.html, the new rule does not preserve a kill-shot list; it removes most of the rows' is asserted for six rows but demonstrated only for two (Sydney-Perth and Polaris). The four PENDING rows (JFK-LHR, SAA, Eclipse, NMP) are not walked through against the three-prong rule, so the 'most of the rows' claim rests on the reader trusting us that the other four would also fail the rule. EXP-193's anticipated_objections #3 explicitly acknowledges this gap and responds: 'The PENDING rows are addressed in their own subsections and remain testable on their own merits — Section 5.0 simply notes that the demoted rows fall out cleanly under the strengthened rule.' That response is reasonable but does not match the actual prose scope — the prose says 'six rows,' not 'the two demoted rows.' This is a quiet mismatch between EXP-193's stated scope and the prose's actual reach, and a careful defender can point to it as rhetorical overreach. In practice: if our Part 5 tests 3-6 (JFK-LHR Std Model Explains, SAA Std Model Explains, Eclipse Std Model Explains, NMP Refuted by Data 39.9%) are the true basis for saying the new rule removes those four rows, paragraph 2 should cite those findings, not just gesture at 'most of the rows.'
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) tighten paragraph 2's scope claim, or (b) add a concrete row-by-row enumeration. Option (a): change 'it removes most of the rows' to 'it removes both of the rows the 2026-04-17 edit already demoted, and — as Tests 3-6 below show — it removes the four remaining rows as well once their verdicts are carried across.' Option (b): after the Sydney-Perth and Polaris sentences, add: 'The four pending rows fare the same under honest application: JFK-LHR and SAA and the August 2026 eclipse are classified "Std Model Explains" in our Part 5 tests 3-5 (i.e., already covered by a competing explanation — failing the third prong), and the NMP drift-rate row is currently at 39.9% error on its own metric (no clean-result discriminator to apply the rule to).' Option (b) is stronger because it does the work in prose; option (a) is cheaper.

holes_found.description: Paragraph 2's penultimate sentence — 'The page changes function from "falsification instrument" to "methodology-of-good-evidence note" — an evidence-quality exhibit, not a globe-knockout case' — is an interpretive reframing, not a factual claim about docs/killshot.html. The dome author did not rename the page, did not relabel the six rows as a methodology exhibit, and did not author a 'methodology-of-good-evidence note' framing. The page is still published at docs/killshot.html with a kill-shot header and six candidate tests. Our prose is characterizing the effect of the rule change the way we want readers to see it, but presenting that characterization as if the author had adopted it. A defender will say: 'No — the page is still titled "Kill-Shot Binary Tests" and still lists six tests. Two rows got softer labels and four rows are pending their test windows. The author\'s intent is that the page is a kill-shot page. You\'re putting words in his mouth about "methodology-of-good-evidence."' The interpretation is defensible but we should signal that it is OUR interpretation, not an observable fact about the page's current framing.
holes_found.recommendation: Soften the framing to signal it is our interpretation. Suggested: 'Under honest application of the new rule, the page functions less as a falsification instrument and more as a methodology-of-good-evidence exhibit — we read it as an evidence-quality exhibit, not a globe-knockout case; the author has not adopted that framing, but the rule he now endorses points there.' This keeps the rhetorical force while avoiding the reification of our interpretation into a claim about the page.

holes_found.description: EXP-193 addresses c1 hole #4 (framing asymmetry) but does NOT integrate c1 stronger_arguments #2 (Popper/Lakatos citation on retrofitted falsification criteria). Paragraph 1 now cleanly credits the new rule as 'methodological strengthening' without acknowledging that retrofitting conditions to a falsification rule AFTER some rows have become embarrassing is itself a recognized degenerative-research-program pattern (Lakatos 1970). Without that citation, paragraph 1 can be quoted by a dome defender as independent critical endorsement of the new rule — 'see, even the critics call this methodological strengthening.' The stronger_arguments #2 recommendation remains open and was the natural pairing for hole #4: crediting the rule methodologically while noting that the timing and occasion of its introduction are themselves a methodological concern would have given paragraph 1 both halves of the honest assessment.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a short parenthetical or clause to paragraph 1 after the 'methodological strengthening' sentence. Suggested: 'Two qualifications on that credit: (i) a rule introduced AFTER some rows have become embarrassing, to retire exactly those rows from the kill-shot list, is itself a well-known methodological concern (Lakatos 1970 on degenerative research programmes); (ii) a rule with no dome-falsifiability clause, as noted above, is a narrower rule than the one it replaces.' This pairs holes #2 and this one into a single paragraph-1 addition and forecloses both the rule-is-just-better reading and the critic-endorses-the-rule reading.

holes_found.description: Paragraph 1's phrase 'close to what a working methodologist would actually write down' is a methodological appeal without a cited standard. A defender can push back: 'Which working methodologist? Name the reference.' The phrase works rhetorically but has no external anchor. Low-cost to harden.
holes_found.recommendation: Either cite a concrete methodology reference (Popper 1959, Cartwright 1983, or a recent handbook-of-scientific-methodology source) to anchor the claim, or soften to 'close to what a careful reviewer would require of any single observation offered as globe-falsifying.' The latter avoids making the implicit appeal-to-authority at all.

holes_found.description: Pre-existing: TLDR of the ks-april-2026-update section still reads 'the demoted rows are precisely the two post-window rows on a six-row page.' SEC-5.0.c1 hole #5 flagged this as slight imprecision ('precisely' is stronger than the data supports because SAA is an ongoing-monitoring row and JFK-LHR is PENDING with insufficient data, so the post-window vs pre-window dichotomy is not perfectly clean across the six rows). EXP-193 was scoped to paragraph-level surgery only and explicitly did not touch the TLDR (tldr_change: false). This is not a new issue introduced by the rewrite — it is a standing finding from c1 that remains open. Flagging here so the decider remembers it was not rolled forward.
holes_found.recommendation: Soften TLDR wording from 'precisely the two post-window rows' to 'exactly the two retrospective / post-window rows' (c1's original wording). Would require a separate EXP or decider self-patch since EXP-193 did not touch the TLDR. Low priority; not blocking.

holes_found.description: The final sentence of paragraph 2 — 'We credit the strengthened standard; we observe that under it, the standard is doing our work.' — is rhetorically strong but logically commits us to the claim that the new rule does in fact remove the four PENDING rows, which paragraph 2 has not demonstrated (see hole #3). If we back down from 'most of the rows' under hole #3, this final sentence also needs to soften or the kicker becomes unsupported.
holes_found.recommendation: Tie this to hole #3 resolution. If the row-by-row enumeration is added (hole #3 option b), this kicker stands as written. If the scope is softened (hole #3 option a), change to 'We credit the strengthened standard; we observe that under an honest application of it, the standard lines up with the conclusions we were already drawing about these rows.'

stronger_arguments.description: A short row-by-row 3-prong table (six rows × three prongs = 18 cells) would make the 'removes most of the rows' claim structurally concrete rather than rhetorical. For each row, mark pass/fail on each prong (pre-registered? mechanically discriminating? not already covered by a competing explanation?) and show the aggregate pass result. This converts paragraph 2's central claim from a narrative assertion into an auditable artifact. The reader can verify for herself that the new rule, applied honestly, removes Sydney-Perth (prong 2), Polaris (prong 3), JFK-LHR (prong 3), SAA (prong 3), Eclipse (prong 3), and leaves only NMP as an ambiguous case (prong 2 arguable, current result failing). A small HTML table of this kind is cheap to add and directly forecloses the hole-#1 'survive' ambiguity and the hole-#3 'most of the rows' scope gap.
sources: raw-text/08-live-power.txt (NMP drift rate 39.9% error, 50% adaptive tolerance)

stronger_arguments.description: Introduce the Lakatosian 'protective belt' framing as the methodological backbone of our critique. Under Lakatos (1970), a research programme is 'degenerative' when its proponents add auxiliary hypotheses or tightening conditions to save the programme from refuting observations, without generating new predictive content. The dome author\'s 2026-04-17 rewrite fits this pattern: two post-window rows (Sydney-Perth, Polaris) had become embarrassing, so the falsification rule was narrowed to remove them as kill-shot candidates, without introducing new predictive content. Our paragraph 1 can credit the new rule AS A RULE while simultaneously situating this specific use of it in the Lakatos framework. This is the methodologically honest version of the critique and is hard for a defender to dismiss as mere ad-hominem.
sources: Lakatos, I. (1970) Falsification and the Methodology of Scientific Research Programmes, in Criticism and the Growth of Knowledge, Cambridge UP, pp. 91-195
Popper, K. (1959) The Logic of Scientific Discovery, §19 (ad hoc rescue)
Worrall, J. (1978) The Ways in Which the Methodology of Scientific Research Programmes Improves on Popper\'s Methodology

stronger_arguments.description: The fact that the author rewrote the rule WHILE demoting the two rows that most plainly violated the rule is a strong form of coincident-editing evidence. If the new rule is a principled methodological improvement, it should be defensible to write it at any time on the author\'s own schedule. If the new rule is a retrofit to protect two specific rows, it would appear in the same commit as those rows' demotion. The actual 2026-04-17 commit 5021eec does both in a single edit — the rule rewrite and the row demotions are in the same commit diff. This is a stronger pattern claim than 'the rule was strengthened for the author and relaxed for the test' (the old c1-critiqued phrasing) and stronger than EXP-193's replacement credit-then-apply structure. Paragraph 2 already touches this in the 'A natural dome-defender objection' paragraph that follows ('the rule and the labels moved together') but does not explicitly frame it as coincident-editing evidence of retrofit.
sources: chg-20260417-1620-006.json (commit 5021eec enumeration of row demotions + rule rewrite in one edit)

kernel_of_truth.description: EXP-193 successfully executes the structural fix that SEC-5.0.c1 hole #4 called for. The old single-sentence framing ('strengthened for the author and relaxed for the test') has been cleanly replaced by a two-paragraph structure: paragraph 1 separately credits the new rule as methodological improvement, paragraph 2 separately applies the rule to the six rows and argues it is a concession in our direction. This is an unambiguously better rhetorical structure than the one it replaced — it no longer reads as punishing the author for adopting a more rigorous rule, and it does the honest thing of crediting what is creditable before arguing what is contestable. That structural improvement is real and should be acknowledged.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: The structural fix is real but the new prose introduces three new exposures that together are arguably more severe than the single hole #4 it resolves. (1) The 'survive' verb in paragraph 2's summary sentence creates an apparent internal contradiction with our own Kill-Shot Verdict Summary table (hole #1, major). (2) Paragraph 1's clean credit for the new rule misses the simultaneous deletion of the dome-falsifiability commitment in the same rewrite (hole #2, major). (3) Paragraph 2 claims 'removes most of the rows' but only demonstrates removal for 2 of 6 rows (hole #3, moderate). None of these are fatal — they are all fixable with minor_edit scope — but the net effect of EXP-193 is to trade one moderate framing hole for several more-specific holes in different places. The kernel of truth is that the structural move was correct; the kernel doesn't save the claim because the execution left new surfaces exposed.

text_fingerprint.verdict: meta-section, no per-row verdict — credits self-correction while preserving dated record

deeper_analysis: EXP-193 is a scope-appropriate response to SEC-5.0.c1 hole #4 — it does the right surgical thing (split credit from application across two paragraphs, keep everything else untouched) and the resulting prose is a material improvement on the 'strengthened for the author and relaxed for the test' kicker that c1 flagged. The section's ps-tldr is intentionally untouched because the TLDR never invoked the strengthened/relaxed framing; that scoping discipline is correct. However, the rewrite's execution leaves two major exposures a dome defender can exploit. The first is semantic: paragraph 2 says 'the rows that survive [the tighter rule] are roughly the ones our review had already classified as Std Model Explains or Refuted by Data.' The natural parse of 'survive the tighter rule' is 'pass the rule and remain kill-shot candidates,' under which our claim is internally inconsistent with our own Kill-Shot Verdict Summary table — rows classified 'Std Model Explains' by us are precisely the rows the new rule should REMOVE under its 'not already covered by a competing explanation' prong, so they cannot also survive the rule. The intended meaning is almost certainly 'the rows the author left untouched in the 2026-04-17 edit are the rows we would remove under honest application of the new rule,' but the prose uses 'survive the tighter rule,' which inverts the semantic direction. This ambiguity actually traces back to c1 hole #4's own recommendation phrasing ('the rows that survive the new rule are roughly the rows we already classified as Std Model Explains or Refuted by Data') — EXP-193 implemented c1 faithfully and inherited the ambiguity. Fix is cheap: replace 'survive' with 'left untouched' or 'not yet re-examined' and commit to the concrete claim about the four PENDING rows. The second major exposure is structural: the old dome rule had TWO explicit commitments (a confirm-direction falsifies globe; a fail-direction falsifies dome). The new dome rule has ONE commitment (what counts as a discriminator), and is silent on what a confirmed or failed discriminator implies for either side. The rewrite simultaneously tightened the inbound standard (which paragraph 1 credits) and deleted the outbound dome-falsifiability commitment (which paragraph 1 is silent on). A dome defender will welcome our uncharacterised credit in paragraph 1 because it lets him retreat to the new rule as if it were symmetric strengthening when it is asymmetric. Fix is also cheap: add one sentence flagging the asymmetry. Third, paragraph 2 asserts 'removes most of the rows' but only demonstrates removal for 2 of 6; a short per-row walkthrough would close that gap and simultaneously fix the 'survive' ambiguity. Fourth, c1's stronger_arguments #2 (Popper/Lakatos citation) was not integrated — this is an open opportunity, not a new hole introduced by EXP-193. Fifth, the 'methodology-of-good-evidence note' reframing is our interpretation presented as the page's new function; soften to signal that. Sixth, 'close to what a working methodologist would actually write down' is an uncited methodological appeal. Net judgement: the rewrite is an improvement on what it replaced, addresses c1 hole #4 correctly, and the remaining exposures are all minor_edit scope. No verdict change. The four demoted-in-a-good-way points (credit given, before/after preserved, CSS mismatch flagged, post-window pattern noted) from c1 are intact. The two new holes (survive ambiguity, dome-falsifiability silence) are each worth a single-sentence edit. The decider can sequence these: fix hole #1 and hole #2 in a single targeted edit to the two new paragraphs, ideally as a new EXP since EXP-193 is already integrated and these are follow-ups to its work, not fixes to it.

citations_verified: Quoted new dome rule text 'genuinely pre-registered, mechanically discriminating, and not already covered by a competing explanation' — matches chg-20260417-1620-006.json rule-body-after verbatim (already verified in SEC-5.0.c1 citation_check; re-verified by inspection of sections.json part5.html)
Quoted old dome rule text 'any single confirmed row falsifies the globe' — matches chg-20260417-1620-006.json rule-body-before (the 'confirm' half) verbatim

Sydney-Perth as calibration to a known railway distance — cross-references Part 5 Test 1 which classifies Sydney-Perth as 'Misleading' on calibration grounds (corroborated by the Kill-Shot Verdict Summary table rows 1 in the same part5.html)

Polaris 0.27° excess inside measurement noise covered by atmospheric refraction — cross-references Part 5 Test 2 which classifies Polaris as 'Not Demonstrated' on refraction grounds (corroborated by the Kill-Shot Verdict Summary table row 2)
citations_failed: No outright failed citations in the EXP-193 replacement prose. The prose is citation-light by design (scope was paragraph-level surgery, not fact-dense new claims).
citations_unchecked: 'close to what a working methodologist would actually write down' — no methodologist is cited. Claim is rhetorical rather than citation-backed; marked unchecked rather than failed because the phrasing does not promise a specific reference.
Lakatos 1970 / Popper 1959 — not cited in the new prose but recommended in stronger_arguments. Marked unchecked because they are absent rather than incorrect.

Dropped dome-falsifiability commitment claim (hole #2) — the before/after rule text in the same section documents this plainly, so the claim is self-supporting from the section's own body. No external citation needed, but our paragraph 1 would benefit from explicitly tying this back to the same before/after table it quotes.
